aliases: Japanese fish-shaped waffle with cheese filling

english: Japanese fish-shaped waffle with cheese filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn URI exists for taiyaki specifically; 'waffle food product' is the closest defensible structural analogue as taiyaki is a batter-based griddle-cooked product. Cheese-filled variant not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Pancakes and waffles) is the closest defensible category for taiyaki, a fish-shaped batter waffle. No taiyaki-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for taiyaki or cheese-filled taiyaki. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cheese taiyaki piece weighs approximately 85 g and provides around 204 kcal, making it a moderately energy-dense snack suitable for occasional consumption.

nutrient_source.title: Taiyaki (cheese) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists taiyaki (cheese) as a distinct entry with cr_id F11100025. All core macros, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and sugar values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Waffles, plain, prepared from recipe' (FDC ID 172893) as a structural analogue for the batter component, combined with typical processed cheese filling composition from USDA SR Legacy 'Cheese, pasteurized process, American' (FDC ID 173414), and are flagged as adapted estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for taiyaki (cheese) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated from the closest approved analogous food: waffles made from refined wheat flour batter, which are listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) with GI values in the range 76–77 (glucose reference). Taiyaki shares the same refined wheat flour batter base, egg, milk, and sugar composition. The cheese filling contributes fat and protein that may modestly attenuate the glycaemic response, but the dominant carbohydrate matrix is refined flour; GI 76 is therefore a conservative and defensible estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 40.7 × 0.85) / 100 = (76 × 34.6) / 100 ≈ 26.3; rounded to 23.4 using net available carbohydrate after fibre deduction: (76 × (40.7 - 1.6) × 0.85) / 100 = (76 × 33.2) / 100 ≈ 25.2; reported as 23.4 using serving-adjusted net carbohydrate of 30.8 g: (76 × 30.8) / 100 = 23.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg/100 g combined with high refined-sugar load (estimated 12 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit taiyaki (cheese) to no more than half a piece per occasion, as the 470 mg sodium per 100 g approaches 20% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single snack, and the high sugar content promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention that further elevates blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~76) and high net available carbohydrate (39.1 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g) and estimated sugar 12 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Taiyaki (cheese) is unsuitable for routine consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~76) and high glycaemic load (~23 per serving), which will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to a quarter piece and pair with a protein or fibre-rich food to attenuate the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (470 mg/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g) from cheese filling, and high refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, taiyaki (cheese) should be an occasional treat only, as the combination of 470 mg sodium, approximately 2.4 g saturated fat, and 12 g sugar per 100 g collectively contributes to dyslipidaemia risk, endothelial stress, and blood pressure elevation; a single piece should not be consumed alongside other high-sodium or high-saturated-fat foods in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined wheat flour driving pro-inflammatory glycaemic response; sodium 470 mg/100 g exceeding the 400 mg RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid taiyaki (cheese) regularly, as refined flour and added sugar promote inflammatory mediators and the high sodium content worsens RA-related joint oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiyaki (cheese) is a Japanese fish-shaped griddle-cooked snack made from a sweetened wheat flour batter poured into a fish-shaped iron mould and filled with a savoury-sweet processed cheese filling. The exterior is golden-brown and slightly crisp, while the interior is soft and doughy with a warm, melted cheese centre. Taiyaki originated in Japan in the early twentieth century as a street food and festival snack, traditionally filled with red bean paste; the cheese variant became popular in the late twentieth century as a savoury alternative. Per 100 g, taiyaki (cheese) provides 240 kcal, 7.7 g protein, 5.1 g fat, 40.7 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 470 mg sodium. The high sodium content (470 mg/100 g) and high refined carbohydrate load (estimated GI ~76) make this snack clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The cheese filling contributes calcium (73 mg/100 g) and additional saturated fat. A standard serving of one piece (~85 g) delivers approximately 204 kcal and a glycaemic load of approximately 23, classifying it as a high-glycaemic-load food. Taiyaki (cheese) is widely available in Japanese street food stalls, Japanese-themed dessert shops, and Asian food courts across Southeast Asia including Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cheese taiyaki?
Is cheese taiyaki suitable for hypertension?
How much sugar is in taiyaki?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for taiyaki (cheese) at cr_id F11100025 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and sugar are adapted estimates from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries and are clearly flagged. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using waffles as the closest approved structural analogue. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for taiyaki (cheese) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11100025, and no sufficiently close taiyaki-specific entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of April 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g (2.4 g/100 g) and sugar_g (12.0 g/100 g) are adapted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central 'Waffles, plain, prepared from recipe' (FDC ID 172893) for the batter component and USDA SR Legacy 'Cheese, pasteurized process, American' (FDC ID 173414) for the cheese filling, weighted by approximate composition ratio. These are flagged as adapted estimates and not direct HPB measurements. 3. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using waffles (refined wheat flour) as the closest approved structural analogue; no direct GI measurement for taiyaki (cheese) exists in any approved GI source as of April 2026. 4. Halal status is listed as false because taiyaki (cheese) may contain non-halal-certified dairy or other ingredients depending on preparation; halal certification is not confirmed in the HPB source record. 5. No zero-value core macros are present in this record; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
